title: Contributing to savingBiocObjects
text: Open issue first anything beyond minor fix — describe think missing wrong, can agree approach write. Fork repository create branch main. Edit vignette vignettes/savingBiocObjects.qmd. file standard Quarto markdown; code chunks reproducible. Preview locally pkgdown: Open pull request main. GitHub Actions workflow render site automatically; deployed preview published merge main.
code: pkgdown::build_site() # or, for just the vignette: pkgdown::build_article("savingBiocObjects")

title: Contributing to savingBiocObjects
text: concrete. Prefer runnable code examples prose-descriptions. State trade-offs. approach include honest disadvantages, just advantages. Keep recommendations date. new Bioconductor package supersedes older approach, note update summary table. Cite packages properly. Use citation("pkgname") link Bioconductor landing page.

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: common question Bioinformatics workflows : save Bioconductor object collaborator can load use , persists reliably across time? answer depends several factors: Longevity: file need readable 5 10 years? Language interoperability: recipient use R, Python, ? Object size: object small enough serialize entirely, contain large -disk arrays? Reproducibility: saved form capture provenance metadata alongside data? vignette walks main options, trade-offs, recommendations common scenarios.

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: simplest approach R’s built-binary serialization. saveRDS() saves single object .rds file; save() bundles one named objects .RData (.rda) file. Prefer saveRDS() use cases. save() / load() silently overwrites object calling environment shares name, common source confusion. main remaining use case save() .rda data files shipped inside R packages (data/). Advantages: Zero setup — works R object. Compression applied automatically (gzip default). Round-trips perfectly: loaded object identical saved one. Disadvantages: R-: Python languages read files without bridge library. Large objects loaded entirely RAM; lazy / -disk access. use : quick sharing R users project, saving intermediate objects pipeline, anything ~1 GB. workflow downloads .rds files remote URL, consider using BiocFileCache cache locally fetched . guarantee S4 object serialized today work older versions Bioconductor. common reasons S4 class defined earlier version, exists definition changed — new slots added, slots renamed removed, infrastructure moved packages. concrete example: recent Bioconductor release, Seqinfo moved GenomicRanges package. GRanges serialized machine newer setup loaded machine older GenomicRanges class definition embedded Seqinfo slot found. reverse also true: older GRanges objects loaded newer session sometimes required updateObject() migrate internal representation: general lesson : can save data format tied particular version Bioconductor — better, tied R — . GRanges, instance, simple TSV often enough: round-trip survives Bioconductor version readable Python command line. alabaster ecosystem (described ) applies principle systematically better support complex objects, using HDF5 JSON underlying storage formats.
code: se <- SummarizedExperiment::SummarizedExperiment(   assays = list(counts = matrix(1:12, nrow = 3)),   colData = S4Vectors::DataFrame(condition = c("A", "A", "B", "B")) )  # Single object saveRDS(se, file = "my_se.rds") se2 <- readRDS("my_se.rds")  # Multiple objects in one file save(se, file = "my_se.RData") load("my_se.RData")  # 'se' appears in the workspace gr <- readRDS("old_granges.rds") gr <- updateObject(gr, verbose = TRUE) # Save write.table(as.data.frame(gr), "ranges.tsv", sep = "\t", quote = FALSE)  # Restore gr2 <- makeGRangesFromDataFrame(   read.table("ranges.tsv", header = TRUE, sep = "\t"),   keep.extra.columns = TRUE )

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: large assay matrices (e.g., single-cell count matrices millions cells), impractical hold entire object RAM. HDF5Array package provides array classes backed HDF5 files, enabling lazy loading --memory computation. saved directory contains HDF5 file assay data .rds file non-assay metadata. Advantages: Assay data stored disk; chunks access read RAM. HDF5 widely used binary format readers Python (h5py, anndata), Julia, C/C++, . Good objects tens gigabytes assay data. Disadvantages: output directory, single file, complicates transfer (use tar zip sharing). .rds envelope metadata still R-specific. Write performance can slower saveRDS() small objects. use : large single-cell spatial datasets want -disk access; workflows shared R users need memory efficiency.
code: library(HDF5Array) library(SummarizedExperiment)  # Save: writes a .h5 file and a small .rds "envelope" saveHDF5SummarizedExperiment(se, dir = "my_se_hdf5/", replace = TRUE)  # Load: the assay data remains on disk until accessed se_h5 <- loadHDF5SummarizedExperiment("my_se_hdf5/")

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: alabaster family packages provides language-agnostic, file-based serialization format. Objects saved directory standard files (HDF5, JSON, CSV) schema can read R, Python, language. alabaster umbrella package pulls support common Bioconductor classes. Individual sub-packages cover specific classes: alabaster.se SummarizedExperiment, alabaster.sce SingleCellExperiment, . Advantages: Truly language-agnostic: Python readers exist via dolomite family packages, enabling seamless R ↔︎ Python interoperability. Built open standards (HDF5, JSON); inspectable without R. Forward-designed long-term reproducibility schema versioning. good choice archives data portals. Disadvantages: Newer ecosystem; Bioconductor classes alabaster support yet. Requires installing relevant alabaster.* sub-package class. Like HDF5Array, output directory. use : archival storage, data portal submissions, cross-language workflows, situation want saved format readable without R. single-cell workflows move R Python, common alternative alabaster zellkonverter, converts directly SingleCellExperiment Python AnnData format (.h5ad files used scanpy related tools). often convenient path collaborators working scanpy, .h5ad native format side. trade-relative alabaster .h5ad AnnData-specific rather general Bioconductor serialization format.
code: library(alabaster.base)  # Save to a directory saveObject(se, path = "my_se_alabaster/")  # Load back se3 <- readObject("my_se_alabaster/") library(zellkonverter)  # SingleCellExperiment -> .h5ad (readable by scanpy in Python) writeH5AD(sce, file = "my_sce.h5ad")  # .h5ad -> SingleCellExperiment sce2 <- readH5AD("my_sce.h5ad")

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: single-cell workflows move R Python, common alternative alabaster zellkonverter, converts directly SingleCellExperiment Python AnnData format (.h5ad files used scanpy related tools). often convenient path collaborators working scanpy, .h5ad native format side. trade-relative alabaster .h5ad AnnData-specific rather general Bioconductor serialization format.
code: library(zellkonverter)  # SingleCellExperiment -> .h5ad (readable by scanpy in Python) writeH5AD(sce, file = "my_sce.h5ad")  # .h5ad -> SingleCellExperiment sce2 <- readH5AD("my_sce.h5ad")

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: object GRanges similar ranges object goal interoperability tools (genome browsers, Python, command-line utilities), exporting BED format often useful R-specific serialization. Two options: BED files store chromosome lengths genome build information, Seqinfo silently dropped export. preserve , write alongside BED file restore load:
code: # rtracklayer — general-purpose genomics I/O library(rtracklayer) export(gr, "ranges.bed")  # plyranges — tidier interface, writes BED3/BED6 depending on the object library(plyranges) write_bed(gr, "ranges.bed") # Save write.csv(as.data.frame(seqinfo(gr)), "seqinfo.csv")  # Restore si <- as(read.csv("seqinfo.csv", row.names = 1), "Seqinfo") seqinfo(gr2) <- si

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: BED files store chromosome lengths genome build information, Seqinfo silently dropped export. preserve , write alongside BED file restore load:
code: # Save write.csv(as.data.frame(seqinfo(gr)), "seqinfo.csv")  # Restore si <- as(read.csv("seqinfo.csv", row.names = 1), "Seqinfo") seqinfo(gr2) <- si

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: Object-level metadata stored metadata(object) — things like processing parameters, provenance notes, experiment descriptors — lost format encodes ranges assay data. applies whether writing BED file, HDF5 matrix, non-R format. Write JSON sidecar file travels data: toJSON handles simple R types (lists, vectors, data frames) well, complex objects (S4 instances, environments) need simplified omitted serializing.
code: library(jsonlite)  # Write writeLines(toJSON(metadata(se), pretty = TRUE, auto_unbox = TRUE),            "metadata.json")  # Read back metadata(se2) <- fromJSON("metadata.json")

title: Saving Bioconductor Objects for Sharing
text: new projects recommend defaulting saveRDS() convenience upgrading alabaster cross-language access archival stability becomes priority. aware R-serialized Bioconductor object may require updateObject() loaded different Bioconductor release.
